paragraphs.text: Five years of operator filings, federal violation logs, and ambient sensor archives — reconciled, audited, and made public. What the regulators won't summarize, we tabulate.
Operator rankings reconcile EPA ECHO violation records with annual 10-K filings; profits are normalized to U.S. operations only, excluding foreign subsidiaries.
State-level methane figures use NOAA GML tower averages, weighted by sensor uptime. Anomalies above 3σ are excluded.
All raw queries, SQL transformations, and reconciliation scripts are published in our open repository.
All tables, sensor logs, and reconciliation notebooks are released under CC BY 4.0.

Federal land violations (BLM jurisdiction) compared against state-level violations for the ten worst-offending facilities. Percentages reflect share of total violations attributable to each site within its jurisdiction.

"The methane numbers aren't ambiguous. They've been measurable since 2016. What's changed is not the science — it's our willingness to call inaction by its name. The instruments don't lie. The enforcement memos do."
    — Dr. Helena Voss, former senior atmospheric scientist, U.S. EPA Region 6

Numbers compress what observation expands. The 847-ppb mean methane figure from the Permian flyover is a single weighted average drawn from 1.4 million sensor pings, and it cannot show you the Loving County ranch where every roofline corrodes faster than expected, or the family in Eddy County whose three asthmatic children share a bedroom 1.2 miles from a leaking flare stack. The tables register the rates. They cannot register what living adjacent to those rates feels like.

But the tables do something else: they refuse the operator's preferred ambiguity. When a company tells a state legislature that emissions are "within expected parameters," our reconciliation script can answer whose parameters, recorded by which sensor, on which day, and at what compliance threshold. We publish every cell. We publish the SQL. We publish the source files. The pages that follow this one — investigation, field notes, the front-page lead — are how those numbers become stories. This is where they begin as evidence.
Independent investigative reporting on environmental collapse, industrial negligence, and climate displacement across the American interior. Reader-funded since 2019.
One letter per week. The week's investigations, raw data, and a field note.

ppbParts per billion — atmospheric concentration unit.
FlaringControlled burn of associated gas at the wellhead.
VentingDirect, unburned release of methane into the atmosphere.
ECHOEPA's Enforcement and Compliance History Online database.
Orphan WellInactive well with no solvent operator on record.
Bond CoverageSurety posted to fund eventual plugging and remediation.
Fugitive EmissionUnintended leakage from valves, flanges, or seals.
Tier-1 ViolationEPA classification: imminent harm to human health.

visibleText: Vol. VI · No. 47 — Data Desk Edition Thursday, October 17, 2024 · Updated 06:14 CT Fracture Line Dispatch Independent reporting from the American interior Front Investigation Data Desk Field Notes About Data Desk · Quarterly Indicators Report The Numbers Behind the Damage Five years of operator filings, federal violation logs, and ambient sensor archives — reconciled, audited, and made public. What the regulators won't summarize, we tabulate. Reporting period: Q3 2019 – Q2 2024 Records analyzed: 1,448,330 Operators tracked: 312 Last refresh: 14 Oct 2024 Headline Indicators 06 / 06 847 ppb Mean methane · Permian flyover 14,210 Sq. mi. affected basin-wide $2.31B Unpaid remediation owed 3,402 Federal violations · FY24 0.04% Fines collected vs. profits 71,490 Residents in 5-mi buffer zone Tables & Rankings 02 datasets Twelve Worst Operators — Fine-to-Profit Ratio, FY 2023 # Operator HQ Fines ($M) Profit ($B) Ratio Tier 01 Helix Petroleum LLC Midland, TX 4.8 11.20 0.04% Critical 02 Sandhills Energy Corp Casper, WY 3.1 8.74 0.04% Critical 03 Bluestem Resources Tulsa, OK 2.9 6.10 0.05% Critical 04 Mesa Vista Operating Albuquerque, NM 5.2 9.88 0.05% Severe 05 Northstar Midstream Williston, ND 6.4 10.42 0.06% Severe 06 Cottonwood Drilling Oklahoma City 1.8 2.92 0.06% Severe 07 Red Mesa Pipeline Farmington, NM 2.2 3.40 0.06% Severe 08 Apex Hydrocarbons Houston, TX 4.0 5.71 0.07% Elevated 09 Plainsong Energy Denver, CO 3.6 4.88 0.07% Elevated 10 Verity Wellhead Inc. Hobbs, NM 2.5 3.21 0.08% Elevated 11 Coyote Basin Gas Vernal, UT 1.4 1.62 0.09% Moderate 12 Iron Hand Petroleum Bakersfield, CA 1.1 1.20 0.09% Moderate Source: EPA ECHO database (Sept 2024 pull), 10-K filings. Ratio = fines paid ÷ net pre-tax profit. Five-Year Methane Trend by State — ppb, regional sensor mean State 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 Δ% Texas 1,902 1,944 2,011 2,098 2,212 +16.3% New Mexico 1,884 1,910 1,975 2,062 2,170 +15.2% Oklahoma 1,861 1,879 1,902 1,944 1,998 +7.4% North Dakota 1,856 1,870 1,915 1,968 2,041 +10.0% Wyoming 1,843 1,860 1,884 1,910 1,952 +5.9% Colorado 1,838 1,855 1,872 1,888 1,910 +3.9% Kansas 1,830 1,836 1,848 1,860 1,879 +2.7% Louisiana 1,872 1,891 1,920 1,966 2,028 +8.3% Source: NOAA GML continuous monitoring network, fixed-tower means. Reported in parts-per-billion. Methodology Operator rankings reconcile EPA ECHO violation records with annual 10-K filings; profits are normalized to U.S. operations only, excluding foreign subsidiaries. State-level methane figures use NOAA GML tower averages, weighted by sensor uptime. Anomalies above 3σ are excluded. All raw queries, SQL transformations, and reconciliation scripts are published in our open repository. Glossary ppb Parts per billion — atmospheric concentration unit. Flaring Controlled burn of associated gas at the wellhead. Venting Direct, unburned release of methane into the atmosphere. ECHO EPA's Enforcement and Compliance History Online database. Orphan Well Inactive well with no solvent operator on record. Bond Coverage Surety posted to fund eventual plugging and remediation. Fugitive Emission Unintended leakage from valves, flanges, or seals. Tier-1 Violation EPA classification: imminent harm to human health. The tables register the rates. They cannot register what living adjacent to those rates feels like. But the tables do something else: they refuse the operator's preferred ambiguity. When a company tells a state legislature that emissions are "within expected parameters," our reconciliation script can answer whose parameters, recorded by which sensor, on which day, and at what compliance threshold.
